excerpt: Learn how to edit audio, voice recordings, songs, and music on PC, Mac, iPhone, Android, and Chromebook with cleanup settings that work.
cardBlurb: A complete walkthrough for editing cleaner audio, fixing voice recordings, correcting noise, editing music, and exporting creator-ready files.
heroDescription: Edit cleaner audio, voice recordings, songs, and music with the right software, effects chain, loudness targets, and practical creator workflows.
seoDescription: Learn how to edit audio, voice recordings, songs, and music with noise removal, EQ, compression, audio correction, and exact export settings.

author.name: Rehan Kadri
author.bio: Rehan Kadri is an SEO specialist, content strategist, and growth marketer with 8+ years of hands-on experience. He started his journey at the age of 14 and has since grown a blog to 1M+ traffic and built an audience of 33K+ subscribers. He helps brands and creators scale through SEO, social media marketing, and data-driven strategies, with deep expertise in YouTube growth.

faqEntries.question: What is the best free audio editing software in 2026?
faqEntries.answer: Audacity is the best free audio editor for PC and Mac — it handles recording, noise removal, EQ, compression, and export in a single, stable application. For mobile, GarageBand (iOS, free) is the gold standard. For online free editing, Adobe Podcast (Enhance Speech tool) is exceptional for voice cleanup with zero technical knowledge required.

faqEntries.question: How do I remove background noise from an audio recording?
faqEntries.answer: In Audacity: (1) Select 2-3 seconds of pure background noise, (2) Go to Effect > Noise Reduction > Get Noise Profile, (3) Select your entire recording, (4) Go to Effect > Noise Reduction and apply with settings around 12dB reduction, 6 sensitivity, 3 frequency smoothing. For AI-powered noise removal with one click, use Adobe Podcast Enhance Speech or NVIDIA RTX Voice (if you have an NVIDIA GPU).

faqEntries.question: What loudness level should I export audio at for YouTube or podcasts?
faqEntries.answer: YouTube normalizes audio to approximately -14 LUFS (integrated loudness). Podcasts typically target -16 to -19 LUFS with a -1 dBTP true peak. For voice recordings and YouTube videos, aim for -14 to -16 LUFS integrated with peaks no higher than -1.5 dBTP. Use a free LUFS meter plugin or Audacity's built-in loudness normalization tool.

faqEntries.question: What audio editing settings work best for voice recordings?
faqEntries.answer: For voice recordings, a standard effects chain is: (1) High-pass filter at 80-100Hz to remove rumble, (2) Noise reduction at 12dB, (3) DeEsser to reduce harsh 's' sounds at 5-8kHz, (4) EQ boost at 2-4kHz for presence and clarity, (5) Dynamic compression at 4:1 ratio with -20dB threshold, (6) Limiter at -1dBTP, (7) Loudness normalization to -14 LUFS. This chain works in Audacity, Adobe Audition, DaVinci Resolve Fairlight, and GarageBand.

faqEntries.question: How do I edit audio on my phone?
faqEntries.answer: On iPhone: Use GarageBand (free) for multi-track editing, voice memos cleanup, and music editing. It's the most powerful free mobile audio editor available. On Android: Use Bandlab (free), WaveEditor Pro, or AudioLab. For simple noise removal and voice enhancement on any phone, Adobe Podcast's Enhance Speech tool works entirely through a web browser — no app download required.

summaryPoints: Audio quality matters more than video quality — poor audio loses viewers in the first 30 seconds.
The three most important edits for any voice recording: noise removal, EQ, and dynamic compression.
Target -14 to -16 LUFS for YouTube; -16 to -19 LUFS for podcasts.
Audacity is the best free audio editor for PC/Mac; GarageBand is best for iPhone.
Adobe Podcast Enhance Speech removes noise and fixes voice recordings in one click — no technical knowledge required.
Cut the silence: editing out pauses, filler words, and dead air improves perceived quality dramatically.
intro: Audio quality is the single most important technical factor in whether people watch your video or listen to your podcast until the end. Research consistently shows that viewers tolerate average video quality but will abandon content within 30 seconds if the audio is difficult to hear, noisy, or echoes. Getting audio right is not a nice-to-have — it is the minimum threshold for being taken seriously as a creator.

This guide covers audio editing for every use case and every skill level: editing voice recordings for YouTube and podcasts, cleaning up music files, using Audacity and GarageBand on desktop and mobile, and achieving professional-sounding results even if you've never opened an audio editor before.

sections.title: Best Audio Editing Software for Every Platform and Budget

blocks.text: Before you start editing, you need the right tool. The best audio editor depends on your device, your budget, and your use case. Here is the definitive breakdown for 2026.

items: Audacity (Free, PC/Mac/Linux): The best free audio editor. Handles noise removal, EQ, compression, multi-track recording, and export. Slightly dated UI but extremely capable.
GarageBand (Free, Mac/iPhone/iPad): Apple's free audio editor is genuinely professional-quality for voice recordings, podcast editing, and music production. Best free option for Mac users.
Adobe Audition (Paid, PC/Mac): Industry standard for podcast and broadcast audio. Best noise removal tools of any DAW. Part of Adobe Creative Cloud ($55/month).
DaVinci Resolve Fairlight (Free audio section): If you edit video in DaVinci Resolve, the Fairlight audio section is a complete professional DAW included for free.
Logic Pro (Mac, $200 one-time): The best audio editor for Mac users who need professional music production capabilities.
FL Studio (PC/Mac, $99+): Best for music production, beat-making, and electronic music editing.

Adobe Podcast Enhance Speech (Free, browser-based): AI-powered voice cleanup tool that removes noise, fixes room echo, and equalizes voice in one click. Works on any device with a web browser — no software download required.
Bandlab (Free, iOS/Android): Best free mobile audio editor for Android. Multi-track recording, mixing, and effects.
CapCut (Free, iOS/Android/PC): Primarily a video editor but has solid audio editing including noise reduction — useful if you're already editing video content.

blocks.title: Start With Adobe Podcast Enhance Speech
blocks.body: If you have a recording that needs cleanup and you have zero audio editing experience, go to podcast.adobe.com/enhance and upload your file. The AI will remove background noise, fix room echo, and equalize your voice automatically. The result is often better than what a beginner would achieve manually in Audacity after an hour of work. Use this tool as a starting point, then do fine-tuning manually if needed.

sections.title: How to Edit Audio in Audacity: Step-by-Step

blocks.text: Audacity is free, works on PC, Mac, and Linux, and can handle everything from simple noise removal to multi-track podcast editing. Here is the complete workflow for editing a voice recording in Audacity.

items: Step 1 — Import your file: File > Import > Audio. Audacity supports MP3, WAV, M4A, AIFF, and most common formats.
Step 2 — Remove silence gaps: Use the Silence Finder (Analyze > Silence Finder) or manually select and delete long pauses.
Step 3 — Apply High-Pass Filter (remove rumble): Effect > Filter Curve or Effect > High Pass Filter. Set the cutoff frequency to 80-100Hz for voice. This removes low-frequency hum and HVAC noise.

Step 4 — Noise removal: Select 2-3 seconds of background-only audio (the 'noise floor'). Effect > Noise Reduction > Get Noise Profile. Then select all audio (Ctrl+A), go back to Effect > Noise Reduction, and apply with settings: 12-15dB reduction, sensitivity 6, frequency smoothing 3.
Step 5 — Apply EQ (boost presence): Effect > Filter Curve. Add a slight boost (+2-3dB) around 2,000-4,000Hz for vocal clarity and presence. Cut slightly (-3dB) at 200-400Hz to reduce muddiness.
Step 6 — Apply Compression: Effect > Compressor. Use ratio 4:1, threshold -20dB, attack 200ms, release 1000ms. This evens out volume differences between louder and quieter parts of speech.
Step 7 — Normalize loudness: Effect > Loudness Normalization. Set target to -16 LUFS for podcasts or -14 LUFS for YouTube. Enable 'Normalize to' and set 'Treat mono as dual-mono: on'.
Step 8 — Apply Limiter: Effect > Limiter (or use the Clipfix effect). Set input gain 0dB, limit to -1.5dBTP to prevent clipping on export.
Step 9 — Export: File > Export > Export as MP3 (for podcasts/distribution) or WAV (for further video editing). Use 320kbps MP3 for maximum compatibility, or 192kbps for smaller file sizes.

sections.title: How to Remove Background Noise from Audio

blocks.text: Background noise is the most common audio problem creators face. Whether it's HVAC hum, keyboard clacking, street noise, room reverb, or a loud fan, there are effective solutions at every level of complexity.

items: Option 1 — Adobe Podcast Enhance Speech (easiest): Upload your file at podcast.adobe.com/enhance. The AI removes noise automatically. Best for voice recordings with consistent background noise.

Option 2 — Audacity Noise Reduction (free): Follow the noise reduction steps above. Most effective for consistent background noise (fans, HVAC, hiss). Less effective for intermittent noise (traffic, dog barking).

Option 3 — NVIDIA RTX Voice (PC with NVIDIA GPU): Real-time noise removal that works during recording and video calls. Completely removes keyboard noise, background voices, and room echo in real time.
Option 4 — Krisp.ai (cross-platform): AI background noise cancellation that works as a virtual microphone. Available for PC, Mac, and during live calls. Free tier allows 60 minutes/week.

Option 5 — iZotope RX (professional, paid): The industry standard for post-production noise removal. Includes tools like De-hum, De-reverb, Dialogue Isolation, and Spectral Repair. RX 11 Elements is $99.

Option 6 — Improve at the source: The most effective noise removal is preventing noise during recording. Close windows, turn off fans, record in a closet (clothes absorb echo), use a directional cardioid microphone, and record close to the mic (6-10 inches).

sections.title: How to Edit Audio on iPhone and Android

blocks.text: Mobile audio editing has become genuinely capable in 2026. The best apps can handle noise removal, multi-track mixing, and professional export entirely from a smartphone — no desktop required.

items: GarageBand (iPhone/iPad, free): The most powerful free audio editor on mobile. Supports multi-track recording, Smart Controls for EQ and compression, and exports to WAV, MP3, and AIFF. Interface is optimized for touchscreen.

Adobe Podcast Enhance Speech (any phone, browser-based): Simply go to podcast.adobe.com/enhance in your mobile browser, upload a recording from your phone's storage, and download the cleaned version. Zero app download required.
Bandlab (Android/iOS, free): Multi-track audio workstation for mobile. Includes a full mixer, effects chain, and noise gate. Best free option for Android users.
WaveEditor (Android, free/paid): Professional waveform editor for Android with noise reduction, EQ, and detailed editing. Good for precise cuts and edits.
CapCut (iOS/Android, free): If you're editing video and audio together, CapCut's audio tools include noise reduction and voice enhancement with one tap.
Dolby On (iOS/Android, free): Records with AI enhancement in real-time, automatically adding noise reduction, EQ, and dynamics processing as you record.

sections.title: Audio Editing for YouTube: Settings and Standards

blocks.text: YouTube applies loudness normalization to all videos upon upload. Understanding YouTube's audio standards will help you export videos at the right levels and avoid the quality degradation that happens when YouTube has to significantly adjust your audio.

items: YouTube target loudness: -14 LUFS integrated (YouTube normalizes all audio to approximately -14 LUFS).
Maximum true peak: -1 dBTP to prevent distortion after YouTube's processing.
Recommended format: Export audio as AAC 192-320kbps, stereo, 44.1kHz or 48kHz sample rate.
If your audio is quieter than -14 LUFS: YouTube will boost the volume, which may amplify background noise.
If your audio is louder than -14 LUFS: YouTube will reduce the volume, which can cause over-compressed audio to sound flat.
The goal is to deliver audio that already matches -14 LUFS so YouTube doesn't need to modify it.
Use a free LUFS meter to check your levels before export: ToneBoosters ISOL8 (free), Youlean Loudness Meter (free), or Audacity's built-in loudness normalization (measures LUFS automatically).

blocks.title: The Fastest Way to Fix YouTube Audio Quality
blocks.body: If your existing videos have poor audio, you don't need to re-edit the entire video. YouTube allows you to replace the audio track in YouTube Studio under the 'Editor' section without re-uploading the video. Re-record your voiceover in a better environment, process it correctly, and swap the audio while keeping all your views, comments, and SEO rankings intact.

sections.title: Audio Editing for Podcasts: Standards and Workflow

blocks.text: Podcast audio standards differ slightly from YouTube. Most podcast platforms (Spotify, Apple Podcasts, Anchor) have their own normalization that targets -16 to -19 LUFS. Here is the complete workflow for producing podcast-ready audio.

items: Target loudness: -16 LUFS integrated (this is the standard most podcast distributors recommend).
True peak: -1.5 dBTP or lower.
Export format: MP3 at 128kbps (mono, voice-only) or 192kbps (stereo, music included), 44.1kHz sample rate.
Remove all long pauses: Edit out any silence longer than 2 seconds.
Edit out filler words: 'Uh,' 'um,' 'like,' and 'you know' — removing these makes the host sound more articulate even if they're not.
Use a noise gate: Automatically silences background noise between speech segments — most DAWs include this as an effect.
Process each speaker on a separate track: If you have multiple guests (recorded via Zoom, Riverside, or Squadcast), each person's audio should be on its own track for individual noise removal and EQ.
Add your intro music: Use copyright-free music from Artlist, Epidemic Sound, or YouTube Audio Library. Fade in at 0dB, then duck (auto-lower) to -20dB when speech begins.
